claim: According to TEM, the macroscopic γ phase is a mixture of γ, fine (Fe,Mn)3AlCx κ-carbides, and (Fe,Mn)3AlCx κ′ phases.
evidence_text: the macroscopic γ phase is a mixture of the γ + fine(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ) + (Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ′ ) phases

claim: The macroscopic α phase consists of D03 and coarse (Fe,Mn)3AlCx κ″ phases.
evidence_text: the macroscopic α phase is a mixture of the D03 + coarse(Fe, Mn)3AlCx (κ′ ′ ) phases

claim: A linear relationship exists between ultimate tensile strength (MPa) and Rockwell C hardness such that UTS = 20.93 × HRC.
evidence_text: the relationship between hardness and ultimate strength is UTS 20.93HRC.

claim: The alloys were melted in an air induction furnace under N₂ atmosphere and poured at 1500–1520°C into investment casting molds preheated at 1160–1180°C, producing sheets of 2.5 mm × 15 mm × 125 mm.
evidence_text: melted in an air induction furnace under the N2 atmosphere protection ... poured at 1500–1520°C into the investment casting preheated at 1160–1180°C with the 2.5 mm × 15 mm × 125 mm thick sheets.

claim: Tensile tests were conducted at room temperature with an initial strain rate of 3.5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ on specimens having a gauge length of 25 mm and a cross‑section of 6.25 mm × 2.0 mm, averaging results from five specimens per condition.
evidence_text: Tensile specimens with a gauge length of 25 mm and a cross section of 6.25 mm × 2.0 mm were machined ... Tensile tests were carried out ... at room temperature, with an initial strain rate of 3.5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and used five specimens to obtain their average.

claim: TEM foils were twin‑jet electropolished in an electrolyte of 60% ethanol, 30% acetic acid, and 10% perchloric acid at –15 to 0°C with a current density of 1.5–2.5×10⁴ A/m².
evidence_text: Specimens for TEM were prepared by means of a double twin jet electropolisher with an electrolyte of 60% ethanol, 30% acetic acid, and 10% perchloric acid. The polishing temperature was kept in the range from −15 to 0 °C, and the current density was kept in the range from 1.5 to 2.5×10⁴ A/m².

claim: Young’s modulus and damping ratio were obtained by experimental modal analysis on cantilever beam specimens, extracting natural frequencies and damping via the half‑power bandwidth method and fitting frequencies by finite element analysis.
evidence_text: The Young’s modulus and damping ratio were examined by the use of experimental model testing method. ... constructed as a cantilever beam structure, ... extracted through the FRF by the half-power point method. Model analysis ... to fit the natural frequencies by adjustment via Young’s modulus.

claim: Optical micrographs (Fig. 1(a)–(h)) of the as-cast sheet alloys reveal dendritic solidification; the α phase (white region) fraction decreases with increasing Mn content, and the 40 mass% Mn alloy (alloy H) is essentially single γ phase.
evidence_text: The typical OMs of the present alloys are shown in Fig. 1(a) through 1(h), revealing the dendritic solidification occurred. Macroscopically, there is a mixture region of α phase (white region) and γ phase. The α phase consisted in the alloys decreases with increasing Mn content, and the alloy with 40 mass%Mn content is essentially a single γ phase.

claim: The relationship between α phase percentage and Mn mass% (neglecting Al and C effects) is α% = 0.0244(Mn%)^2 - 2.744(Mn%) + 68.65, with an error of approximately 0.93%.
evidence_text: Neglected the effects of the aluminum and carbon contents, it could be also found that the ratios between the percentage of the α phase and Mn mass% contents for various alloys is α% = 0.0244(Mn%)^2 - 2.744(Mn%) + 68.65 with the error of about 0.93%.

claim: In the austenite region of alloy C, TEM reveals κ' carbides (coarse) and κ carbides (extremely fine), both identified as (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbides with an ordered L′12-type structure and lattice parameter a = 0.383–0.386 nm.
evidence_text: Therefore, from these superlattice spots and the fact that superlattice 100 spots are much stronger than the 110 spot, it is deduced that the precipitates are the (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbides having an order L′12-type structure with the lattice parameter a = 0.383-0.386 nm.

claim: Dark-field TEM imaging using the 100 κ′ reflection shows that κ′ carbides and κ carbides have the same orientation within the austenite matrix.
evidence_text: Shown in Fig. 3(d) is a 100 κ′ dark-field (DF) electron micrograph, exhibiting the presence of κ′ carbides and both the κ′ carbides and κ carbides have the same orientation.

claim: The coarse κ″ particle in the α region (Fig. 2) is a (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbide with an ordered L12-type structure and lattice parameter a = 0.387–0.389 nm, deduced from equal intensities of superlattice 100, 120 and 110 spots.
evidence_text: Figure 4 shows a SADP taken from the coarse particle marked as κ″ in Fig. 2, it is found that the particle is also a (Fe,Mn)3AlCx (represented as κ″) carbide, same as observed in the matrix. However, from the same strength of superlattice 100, 120 and 110 spots, it is deduced that the particle is having an ordered L12-type structure with the lattice parameter a = 0.387–0.389 nm.

claim: The α phase matrix in all Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys possesses an ordered D03-type structure with lattice parameter a = 0.586–0.588 nm.
evidence_text: From these superlattice spots, it is deduced that the α matrix has an order D03-type structure with the lattice parameter a = 0.586-0.588 nm.

claim: The orientation relationship between the D03 matrix and the κ″ phase is [1-11]κ ∥ [2-20]D03 and (011)κ ∥ (001)D03, as determined by SADP.
evidence_text: Figure 6 is a SADP taken from the D03 matrix and the particle κ″., indicating that the orientation relationship between the D03 matrix and the κ″ phase is [1-11]κ ∥ [2-20]D03 and (011)κ ∥ (001)D03.

claim: Dark-field TEM images of the D03 matrix using g=111 and g=200 reveal a/4⟨111⟩ and a/4⟨200⟩ anti-phase boundaries (APBs); the smaller a/4⟨111⟩ APBs indicate that the high-temperature disordered α phase transformed to D03 domains via a α→B2→D03 continuous ordering transition during quenching.
evidence_text: Figures 7(a) and (b), two 111 and 200 D03 DF electron micrographs, clearly exhibit the presences of a/4⟨200⟩ and a/4⟨111⟩ anti-phase boundaries (APDs), respectively. From the fact that the a/4⟨111⟩ APDs are small than the a/4⟨200⟩ APDs, it indicates that the crystal structure of the matrix at high temperatures should be a disordered α phase and the D03 domains were formed during quenching through a α→B2→D03 continuous ordering transition.

claim: The presence of satellites in SADPs around (200) and (220) reflections suggests that the (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbides formed within the austenite matrix during cooling by spinodal decomposition or chemical fluctuations.
evidence_text: In the Figs. 3(b) and (c), it is also found that satellites could be observed along 100 reciprocal lattice direction around the (200) and (220) reflections spots. The existence of the satellites suggests that the (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbides were formed within the austenite matrix during cooling by spinodal decomposition or the chemical fluctuations were occurred between the (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbides and the austenite matrix.

claim: The overall as-cast microstructure of all alloys except the 40Mn alloy is a mixture of (γ+κ+κ′) and (D03+κ″) regions; alloy H (40 mass% Mn) is fully austenitic (single γ phase).
evidence_text: To sum up, the microstructure of the various Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C sheet castings is a mixture of (γ+κ+κ′) region and (D03+κ″) region and only the alloy with 40% Mn content is a single γ phase.

claim: TEM observation shows that the amount of κ″ precipitates along the γ/α boundaries is higher than within the D03 matrix.
evidence_text: Moreover, based on TEM examination, it would be observed the amount of κ″ precipitates along the γ/α boundaries, as shown in Fig. 2, are higher than within the D03 matrix.

claim: Young's modulus and damping ratio were determined by experimental modal testing of cantilever beam specimens; natural frequencies and damping ratios were extracted from frequency response functions using the half-power point method, and Young's modulus was fitted via finite element analysis.
evidence_text: The Young’s modulus and damping ratio were examined by the use of experimental model testing method. Eight types of sheet castings were constructed as a cantilever beam structure, and tested to obtain the system frequency response function (FRF). The natural frequencies and damping ratios can then be extracted through the FRF by the half-power point method. Model analysis of the cantilever beam is also performed via finite element analysis to fit the natural frequencies by adjustment via Young’s modulus.

claim: The α phase and D03 domains formed during quenching through a continuous α→B2→D03 ordering transition.
evidence_text: α phase and the D0₃ domains were formed during quenching through a α B2 D0₃ continuous ordering transition.

claim: In the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys, the α region is entirely D03 phase containing coarse κ″ precipitates.
evidence_text: the α region of the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys is all the D0₃ phase containing some coarse κ″ phase precipitates.

claim: The microstructure of the various Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C sheet castings is a mixture of (γ+κ+κ′) and (D03+κ″) regions, and only the alloy with 40% Mn content is a single γ phase.
evidence_text: the microstructure of the various Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C sheet castings is a mixture of (γ+κ+κ′) region and (D03+κ″) region and only the alloy with 40% Mn content is a single γ phase.

claim: The amount of κ″ precipitates along the γ/α boundaries is higher than within the D03 matrix.
evidence_text: it would be observed the amount of κ″ precipitates along the γ/α boundaries, as shown in Fig. 2, are higher than within the D03 matrix.

claim: The as-cast Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy sheet casting possesses maximum ultimate tensile strength of 887 MPa and yield strength of 824 MPa.
evidence_text: With the 15 mass%Mn content, the alloy sheet casting possesses maximum UTS = 887 MPa and Y.S. = 824 MPa.

claim: The hardness of the alloys ranges from HRC 31 to 44, and the relationship between UTS and hardness is nearly UTS = 20.93 HRC with an error of approximately 2.1%.
evidence_text: the hardness of the present alloy are in the range of HRC 31–44, and the relationship between the strength and hardness is nearly UTS ≡ 20.93 HRC with the error about 2.1%.

claim: Compared with the microstructures, the (D03+κ″) two-phase region plays an important role in the fracture mechanism.
evidence_text: Compared with the microstructures of the alloys, it implies that the (D03+κ″) two-phase region would play an important role to the fracture mechanism.

claim: The natural frequencies of the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy sheets with Mn contents from 5.12 to 39.73 mass% range from 226 to 242 Hz.
evidence_text: The natural frequencies of the Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy containing Mn of 5.12, 9.85, 14.89, 20.06, 24.87, 30.45, 34.88, 39.73 mass%, with various thickness are 226, 238, 232, 226, 236, 234, 242 and 242 Hz, respectively.

claim: The as-cast microstructure consists of (γ+κ+κ′) and (D03+κ″) regions; the (D03+κ″) phase fraction decreases with increasing Mn content, and at 40 mass% Mn the alloy becomes a macroscopic single γ phase.
evidence_text: The microstructures of the Fe–10Al–(5–40)Mn–1.0C alloy sheet castings are a mixture of the (γ+κ+κ′) regions and the (D03+κ″) regions, and the (D03+κ″) phase region will decreases with the Mn content increased. With 40 mass% Mn content, the microstructure of the alloy would be a macroscopic single γ phase.

claim: The κ or κ′ carbides have an ordered L'12-type structure and are formed during quenching presumably by spinodal decomposition involving chemical fluctuations between the carbides and the austenite matrix.
evidence_text: the κ or κ′ phase has an ordered L⁴ 12-type structure, and formed during quenching by, presumably, spinodal decomposition, and the chemical fluctuations were occurred between the (κ+κ′) carbides and the austenite matrix.

claim: The κ″ carbide has an ordered L12-type structure, with a carbon content lower than that of κ′ and κ carbides, and exhibits a more random disorder distribution.
evidence_text: the κ″ carbide has an ordered L₁₂-type structure, and the carbon solution of the κ″ carbides would be lower than the κ′ and κ carbides, and being more random disorder distribution.

claim: The α ferrite matrix attains an ordered D03 structure during quenching via a continuous ordering transition α→B2→D03.
evidence_text: The α matrix has an order D03-type structure formed during quenching through a α → B2 → D03 continuous ordering transition.

claim: The orientation relationship between D03 matrix and κ″ phase is [1̄11]κ ∥ [220]D03 and (011)κ ∥ (001)D03.
evidence_text: The orientation relationship between the D03 matrix and the κ″ phase is [1 \bar{1} 1]κ ∥ [2 2 0]D03 and (0 1 1)κ ∥ (0 0 1)D03.

claim: The Fe–10Al–(5–40)Mn–1.0C as-cast sheet alloys exhibit hardness HRC 31–44, UTS 646–887 MPa, Y.S. 575–824 MPa, elongation 16.3–29.2%, damping ratio 0.0603–0.0417, and Young's modulus 139–165 GPa.
evidence_text: The hardness, UTS, Y.S., elongation, damping ratio and Young’s modulus of the Fe-10Al-(5·40)Mn-1.0C alloys were in the range of HRC 31–44, 646–887 MPa, 575– 824 MPa, 16.3–29.2%, 0.0603 and 0.0417, and 139–165 GPa, respectively.
